This is a pubcast shot. I'm John Loomer. Beware of advice from fake advertising experts. Let's go. So on this episode, I want to lay out a bit of a warning. There are generally five characteristics of these loud and abrasive supposed experts that you should watch out for. So let's get to it. First, they speak in absolutes. Now, I've been doing this for a really long time, going on 15 years now. While the features and strategies have changed, one thing remains true. It depends. Now, what I mean by that is that there are few absolutes in meta advertising. What works for me may not work for you. So many factors impact the results that you can get and the approach that you should take, like industry and budget and location and brand reputation and your product and on and on. So beware of advertisers who confidently claim that there is one best way to do things that will work for everyone or won't work. It's an attempt to make something easier to understand by suggesting that something is always the case, whether it's the type of optimization or targeting or creative testing or whatever. Now, I openly admit that I probably frustrate people when I'm unwilling to provide universal and

## [03:04 - 06:04]

specific advice that will apply to all businesses all the time. That's just not how it is. So absolutely no absolutes. Second, these loud voices demand a specific campaign structure. So in most cases, these advertisers swear by a super complex approach. If you get good results, do whatever you did to get those results. So whatever. But it's important to understand why there are so many loud voices advocating for specific, complicated campaign structures. This is something that they can brand and name and be known for. them. And finally, they rely heavily on remarketing. So these advertisers focus on their results because results from remarketing often look impressive. They're also misleading.
